reviews.review_text:
[h1]8.5/10 [/h1]
The story keeps you playing, game loop is very good. Looks easy gets difficult towards the end. Really good game that doesn't need perfect graphics to be good. The game doesn't overstay.

It's tough to describe how fun and interesting this game is. It's essentially a job simulator. But it rises well above that, using its medium to tell a particular story of political intrigue. The player can make different decisions which culminate into wildly different endings. 

It's a great game, but there is a barrier to entry. It sat unused in my Library until I got an injury and had a load of extra time because I didn't want to memorize the Issuing Cities of various fake countries. But then I did. And it was well worth it.
one of my favorite games and carried my childhood and very fun ty chat

Are you a fan of authoritarianism, bureaucracy or economic hardship? If so, then this game is perfect for you! With progression comes more rules that really starts to complicate things and test your ability to multi-task. An interesting challenge with a very bleak setting.

Papers, Please — отличная и во многом образцовая игра инди-жанра. Она удивительным образом превращает, казалось бы, унылый бюрократический процесс проверки документов в увлекательный игровой опыт. Антиутопический сеттинг здесь не просто служит фоном: он одновременно развлекает, держит в напряжении и временами по-настоящему трогает игрока.
Игровая механика становится источником пусть и нечастых, но крайне запоминающихся ситуаций, в которых моральный выбор ощущается особенно остро. К 2026 году игра не только прекрасно сохранилась, но и приобрела новую актуальность на фоне происходящих в мире событий.

can be difficult but so fun to pick your side, make moral decisions, and just check people to make sure they are supposed to be there. Has real consequences, political turmoil and so much more all based off your decisions. VERY fun game have had it dang near since it came out and keep coming back.

9/10
Super entertaining gameplay loop. The readjustment to the entry requirements each day keeps the game feeling refreshingly rhythmic.

I love this game so much. The organisation aspect is super satisfying as well as the noises. The chance to be a strict rule follower or a kind human gives for intense moments. So many endings makes all the different options more intense
this game is really fun, even if it is a loop all of the different story elements in each day ensure its never boring. the newspapers are a perfect lesson in propaganda.
the original, great overall even if it can get boring after replaying it too much

Tells a very compelling story with moral, social and philosophical dilemmas that have considerable depth to them. It does an excellent job at portraying the human condition and the inner lives of people you may or may not know. Definitely not something you see every day.
Simple concept but extremely clever. Engaging gameplay. I appreciate the several different possible endings and the daily save points.
Really cool game. It does get a bit more challenging over time, but it also gets easier at the same time,

This game makes you think a lot fun game hard to understand at first but when you finally understand everything just clicks like puzzle pieces something like that or Legos fun game keeps you in your toes

It's fine, definitely very overrated over ten years later. No one ever seems to mention that the careful tone and setting of the game gets totally destroyed by the end, ruining whatever message it could have told.

I don't know what else I can say that hasn't been said a million times by now, but, my god, how I love this game. I had no idea when I bought this that this was the same guy/dev as The Return of the Obra Dinn. What a legacy. This game is endlessly replayable and ticks all my Type-A buttons, lol. Lucas Pope, you're one hell of a game developer. Please never stop making games. You clearly know what you're doing.

All the checkpoint/inspection games that are now on Steam owe their origins to this masterpiece. What I find truly crazy about this game, is it's ability to map my mental degradation as our world became more brain-rotted. 10 years ago I would have to quit endless mode because I was playing it too long, now I don't have that problem. 10/10 Lucas Pope is an artist.

- Be me,
-Load into papers please
-Guards were blown up and never seen again
-I shoot another dude running
-I misclick and shoot a civilian
- me and my family is in jail
- Did i mentioned i intentionally killed my mother in law. son, and uncle?
-10/10

Atmosphere of this game is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ top notch, and I often find myself coming back to it every 6 months or so.

The gameplay style might be monotonous to some less-detail oriented people, but for someone like me it's pretty fun.

Honestly like an 8/10 for the atmosphere. Gameplay is like 6/10 imo.

Even though the gameplay seems mundane on the surface, there are a lot of things in this game that can stress you out, including having to pay very careful attention and make sure that every single detail matches up, the time limit encouraging you to work quickly to make more money (even at the risk of getting written up for missing a discrepancy), and quite a few moments where you have to make some very tough decisions between whether to do what's right or to do what you're told.

At first I saw this game and thought, "Hmm, Imma ply it to chill.". Five min later I'm making the hardest choices I've ever made in my life, whether it's to maitain my job, or it's to deny someone for the most insane things.

Pretty nice game. I love the fact that our decisions change the game.
I wish it could have been a bit longer besides the fact that theres about 20+ endings.
Theres literally no need for anything else really the game is actually amazing and easy as much as its fun.
You need about 2 3 hours to get the games idea and what to do the rest is just learning the details.
You guys can play this game on any device is what Im guessing its pretty cheap to play and enjoy the story line. 
Never crashes and never had any technical issues since i bought it years ago.
Fun, not overly complicated. Make sure you are in the mood to analyse things though.

What would seem like the tedious parts of the game (i.e. flipping through citizens' documents, essentially being a glorified toll booth worker) are actually a fun puzzle. There's a lot of replayability value, as you can get different endings depending on who you decide to let through the checkpoint.

an amazing game, play it now

glory to arstotzka inspector

1.Saved all income for my self
2. Made someone wait more than a hour doing nothing useful while denying everyone
Lost my family,my job 
I recommend this game
Best ten bucks of my life

This game is so much fun. It starts out fairly simple, but as you reach day 12 it starts to ramp up in difficulty lol so far I have played about 6.5 hours and have completed ONE ending (not the one I was trying for lol).

Pros: 
- Always something to look at/something to do, not slow paced
- Makes you think and use your brain
- There are 20 endings! 
- The art style for this game is perfection and fits very well with the gameplay

Cons:
- Not the type of game you can relax and play; you HAVE to be on your A game.
- That's literally the only con I can think of lol

All in all, 10/10 recommend if you're someone looking for a bit of a challenge (and looking to burn a few hours EASILY because there's not really a good "stopping point" lmao it's so addicting)

PS: Glory to Arstotzka!

I first saw this game streamed on Twitch by Ray Narvaez, Jr. in 2018 (I had to go back and check the date), and I was hooked. I don't think I actually ordered the game until the next year. I unlocked all twenty endings and all the achievements. Well worth the buy.
fun and challenging. multiple endings

Papers, Please is a puzzle game first and foremost. Your task is to check peoples' papers as border control, and make sure there are no discrepancies. While this sounds easy in theory, there are a lot of information to cross-check and as the story progresses, you have more and more documents to check. There are also a lot of different documents required based on specific cases, so you have to remember whether you were given all of them.

On top of this, you don't have time to waste. Each day is only a certain length, and you need to manage your savings and expenses. You get paid every day based on the amount of people you have correctly assessed.

If you like puzzles based around memorization and speed, I can not recommend this game highly enough. It is a great challenge and the endless mode is endless fun for anyone who enjoys the concept.

The endless mode features three categories. Each category has four difficulties and a public rank. Options are Timed, where you have 10 minutes to process as many people as you can. Perfection, where you lose as soon as you make a mistake. And Endurance, where you play until your balance gets negative. You get 1 or 2 points per correctly processed person, and a multiple of -5 for each mistake you make.

Another important aspect of this game is the story. Basically, this is a game about war and political tensions. The country you live in seems to be authoritarian. Overall, the atmosphere reminds of the USSR.

This also means that there is a lot of moral decisions to make. There are recurring characters and scripted interactions that ask you to decide between receiving a citation with a penalty for helping someone, or just following the rules.

There are 30 endings in total, and each day acts as a checkpoint, so you're not forced to grind through the entire game 30 times just to see each outcome.

Personally, I think the story is a bit half-baked, as it seems to sometimes conflate authoritarianism with anti-capitalism, without acknowledging that people suffer under the same problems in the western world.

But despite that, I would recommend this game, as the gameplay is very fun, even including the story mode.

Very interesting game, my family died on the third day from sickness and lack of heating in my arstotzkan appartment, after only one week on the job i had to defuse a bomb. Would reccomend. Glory to Arstotzka!!!
Easily one of the most unique games I have ever played. This game is what you show when people ask what's a 10/10 game.

Honestly, this game is creepy, unsettling, difficult and kind of weirdly enjoyable and stressful. It's a spot the difference mechanic, nad sometimes you have to choose, you have to be careful with it. But it's like yeah aftaer a while I just don't really like where this is going and I guess I'm going to take a break. The story and mechanic is relaxing the overall gaem is not. I would recommend it to anyone to at least try as to how kind of unsettling and difficult it is to be an inspector of the same stuff of repttiveness where you largely see things blur together.
an amazing and intresting storys in a small game. Great diffeculty scaling and generlly good gameplay

my attention span equivalent to a toddler with the vast knowledge of italian creatures generated by the computer and this game still forever peak to me 11.5/10 not 12 because my family died (probably) first try
Great story, addicting gameplay, an endless mode, 20 different endings, all gift-wrapped in an indie love project at an affordable price. Incredible.
Game seems boring but its actually pretty fun and has a great story
even tho this game is kidna old now, its still really fun and if you havent ever played this i recommend it

Honestly, can't think of many games like this. As the game goes on, it does get more complex, but you do start to memorise what is where, and what errors look like, which does make it easier. At times can be difficult, but is still an enjoyable game with a good story and well-done pixel art.
Also Jorji Costava makes the game a 20/10, nicest guy in game history.
Who knew a game about checking paperwork could be so good? Anyways, this is first game I've got 100% achievements on!
One of my all time favourite games. I honestly don't have anything negative to say. It's a fun game and you should definitely play it.

Paper's Please was a pleasant game through and through honestly. A very good story and a very fun loop. 

Gameplay wise its a very satisfying deduction game with trying not to get any missed points along with the ever increasing moral dilemma of the story. 

Great play and quite short so I don't need to talk too much on it, I'd recommend picking it up if you've somehow never seen it before. 

Not enough Jorji though.

7.8/10

A simple, fun game that quickly gets complex and challenging, in very interesting ways. I've played through several times and enjoy the various endings to the game. Love the level of detail and highly recommend.

Papers, Please really clicked with the part of my brain that enjoys organizing things and double-checking details. I had a lot of fun with it, but I think you should really consider if that kind of gameplay is for you. There isn't a whole lot to this experience other than your time checking passports in the booth. I enjoyed it.

Objectively well-made with a solid gameplay loop, and did most of what it intended to tonally, but there isn't much weight to making immoral choices when they aren't really... choices. 

Trying to go against the churn of the machine results in your family dying, okay, great, that's a good player consequence. But my family is two textboxes labelled "Son" and "Wife". I would have loved to be shown their faces. There's no point in it after that, anyways, because you quickly reach a game-over for yourself if you keep going. Being railroaded into immorality to play could have been cool, but after a certain point it just becomes a depressing slog.

[code]
[h2]Papers, Please: The Ultimate High-Fidelity Cybernetic Network of Sociological and Bureaucratic Equilibrium[/h2]
Papers, Please is not merely a dystopian puzzle game or a document-checking simulator; it is a stunning, high-fidelity mechanical and political ecosystem where your border checkpoint and the surrounding totalitarian state function as a living, breathing macro-organism. For anyone who appreciates the unyielding beauty of systemic automation, rigorous optimization, and granular structural dynamics, this game provides an elite blueprint for transforming an entropic, chaotic human influx into a perfectly synchronized masterpiece of data-driven execution.

[h2]The Biopolitical Anatomy of a Human Filtration Engine[/h2]
When you operate the stamp and analyze the passport grids of Arstotzka, you aren't just processing citizens, you are engineering a complex, self-sustaining metabolic engine:
[list]
[*]The Document Verification Protocols act as the Primary Immunological Response. The strategic cross-referencing of entry permits, work passes, and biometric data across shifting regulations mimics the cell's precise membrane gating, neutralizing external systemic threats and volatile anomalies with absolute vector precision.
[*]The Inspection Tools and M.O.A. Guidelines function as Metabolic Organs. Watching raw, unverified human inputs enter your interrogation matrix to be synthesized into hyper-complex, error-free approvals or detentions is identical to cellular metabolism converting raw substrates into pure, high-yield systemic stability. Balancing your processing speed and error margins is a brilliant exercise in optimizing synaptic saturation and reaction velocities (Vₘₐₓ).
[*]The Daily Wage and Family Vitality Infrastructure operates as the ultimate Central Nervous System. Whether you are conducting swift document comparisons or managing the strict, multi-phased heat-or-medicine balance at the end of each shift, you are directing the synaptic impulses of a survival loop. The system demands total thermodynamic equilibrium; a single calculation error or citation penalty triggers a localized financial shock that completely dismantles your family's biological homeostasis.
[/list]
[h2]Absolute Sovereign Bureaucratic Autonomy[/h2]
The game completely discards artificial, hand-holding moral dichotomies, presenting a pristine sandbox governed entirely by rigid geopolitical codes, chronological constraints, and mathematical pressure. It respects your analytical intelligence by forcing you to operate as a selective, semi-permeable boundary against external chaotic forces, organizing political and moral chaos into a beautifully sterile, highly rational masterpiece of absolute regulatory engineering.

[h2]The Verdict[/h2]
[i]Papers, Please[/i] is an absolute [b]10/10[/b] masterwork of pure logistical and mechanical optimization. It perfectly captures the transcendent intellectual satisfaction of acting as the hyper-focused nucleus of a self-sustaining bureaucratic apparatus. If you possess a mind for deep data verification, tactical macro-management, or rigorous resource pipelining under extreme duress, this is an indispensable, legendary titan in your [b]Steam[/b] library.
[/code]
